aliases: Crispy fried frog with fish sauce glaze

english: Crispy fried frog with fish sauce glaze

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general frog meat food product class; no specific entry for Vietnamese fish-sauce-glazed fried frog exists. Closest defensible anchor used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers frog legs as a commodity; no specific prepared/fried-with-fish-sauce variant exists. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for fried frog with fish sauce as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of fried frog with fish sauce weighs approximately 300 g, typically comprising fried frog leg pieces coated in a savoury fish sauce glaze, served with steamed rice or as a standalone dish.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Frog legs, raw (FDC ID 174474) combined with FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table for fish sauce contribution
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Vietnamese fried frog with fish sauce exists in any single approved source. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for raw frog legs (FDC ID 174474) with adjustments for deep-frying (oil absorption ~8–10 g fat per 100 g cooked product) and fish sauce glaze contribution to sodium (~400–600 mg/100 g). Input values from the source record (protein 15 g, fat 11.67 g, carbohydrate 5 g, energy 183 kcal, sodium 600 mg per 100 g) are consistent with this estimation and are retained. FAO/INFOODS SEA table consulted for regional fish sauce sodium benchmarks.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate based on mixed protein-fat-dominant fried meat dish with minimal starch coating
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese fried frog with fish sauce. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogy with other deep-fried protein-dominant dishes with minimal starch coating (e.g., fried chicken with light batter, GI ~35–40 per University of Sydney GI Database). Carbohydrate content is low (5 g/100 g), primarily from a thin cornstarch coating and fish sauce sugar. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (35 × 15 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 5.25, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 600 mg per 100 g driven by fish sauce glaze
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, exceeding the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg in a single dish; individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce or a low-sodium fish sauce substitute and limit portion size to 100 g or less.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5 g/100 g) and low glycemic load, but high fat (11.67 g/100 g) from deep-frying and elevated sodium
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate content (5 g/100 g) and low glycemic load (GL 5.25 per 300 g serving) are favourable for glycaemic control, but the high fat from deep-frying and 600 mg/100 g sodium may worsen insulin resistance and fluid retention; individuals with type 2 diabetes should prefer a grilled or pan-fried preparation with reduced fish sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (600 mg/100 g), elevated total fat (11.67 g/100 g) including saturated_fat (2.67 g/100 g), and cholesterol (60 mg/100 g) from deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of high sodium, saturated fat, and cholesterol in a 300 g serving poses a significant cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid deep-fried preparations and request grilled frog legs with minimal fish sauce to reduce sodium and fat load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Frog with Fish Sauce (Vietnamese: Ếch Chiên Nước Mắm) is a popular Vietnamese dish consisting of frog legs that are marinated, coated in a light cornstarch batter, deep-fried until golden and crispy, then tossed in a savoury glaze made from fish sauce, garlic, chilli, sugar, and black pepper. The dish delivers a satisfying contrast of textures — a crispy exterior giving way to tender, mildly flavoured frog meat that is often compared to chicken in taste and texture. It is commonly served as a main dish with steamed jasmine rice or as a sharing plate in Vietnamese restaurants and street food settings. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 183 kcal, 15 g protein, 11.67 g fat (including 2.67 g saturated fat), and 5 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 600 mg driven by the fish sauce glaze. The glycemic index is estimated at 35 (low), making it relatively favourable for blood sugar management despite the frying method. Clinically, the high sodium content is the primary concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease, while the high fat from deep-frying warrants caution for those managing cardiovascular risk or insulin resistance. The dish is not suitable for individuals on low-sodium diets without significant modification.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Vietnamese fried frog with fish sauce exists in any single approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for raw frog legs with adjustments for deep-frying oil absorption and fish sauce sodium contribution. The input values are consistent with this estimation and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database benchmarks for similar fried protein-dominant dishes. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this Vietnamese fried frog with fish sauce preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA table, or Singapore HPB database as of 2026. The input source (GrabFood, ai_generated, confidence 0.7) is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 and has not been used as primary evidence; values have been independently validated against USDA FoodData Central frog legs data and FAO/INFOODS SEA fish sauce benchmarks. Halal status is marked false as frog is not universally considered halal under Islamic jurisprudence (Shafi'i school permits it; Hanafi school prohibits it); consumers should verify with their religious authority. The ethnic_tags in the input (Pho, Banh Mi, Noodle) are not applicable to this dish and have been removed. country_of_origin in the input was listed as Singapore, which is incorrect for this Vietnamese dish; corrected to Vietnam.
